title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Remote Work on Urban Life

texts.A: The mass migration to remote work is a death knell for the city center. Without the daily influx of office workers, the ecosystem of small businesses—cafés, dry cleaners, newsagents—that depended on them is collapsing. We are left with 'ghost downtowns' that are becoming increasingly unsafe and economically stagnant. While some celebrate the 'freedom' of working from home, they overlook the loss of the 'watercooler effect'—the spontaneous collaboration that only happens in a shared physical space. The city was built for proximity, and without it, it loses its soul.
texts.B: The decline of the central business district is not a tragedy, but an opportunity for urban renewal. For decades, our cities have been designed around the needs of the commuter, leading to empty streets at night and exorbitant rents. Remote work allows us to turn these office blocks into affordable housing. I share the view that the 'ecosystem' is changing, but I believe it is moving to the suburbs and smaller towns, revitalizing local communities that were previously 'bedroom suburbs.' We are seeing a more balanced distribution of economic activity.
texts.C: I agree that the 'watercooler effect' is a romanticized concept that rarely led to actual innovation. Most office work is performed in isolation anyway. However, I share the concern that remote work is creating a 'digital divide' in our cities. The wealthy can work from their home offices, while service workers are still forced to commute to increasingly hollowed-out centers. The real challenge is to ensure that the 'revitalization' of smaller towns doesn't lead to a new wave of gentrification that displaces the very people who live there.
texts.D: The debate over remote work focuses too much on 'where' we work and not 'how' we live. Remote work is the ultimate environmental tool; it eliminates millions of tons of carbon from commuting. While I share the skepticism regarding the 'death' of the city, I believe cities must adapt by becoming '15-minute' hubs of leisure and culture rather than engines of productivity. I disagree with those who fear for the small businesses of the center; those that adapt to the new resident population will thrive. The city is not dying; it is finally becoming a place for people instead of workers.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the fate of city centers in the age of remote work?
questions.explanation: Expert A sees a 'death knell' and 'ghost downtowns.' Expert B sees an 'opportunity for urban renewal' and a way to create affordable housing.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's opinion about the positive shift toward local community revitalization?
questions.explanation: Expert B speaks of 'revitalizing local communities.' Expert C also mentions the 'revitalization of smaller towns' (though they warn about gentrification).

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to C regarding the myth of 'spontaneous collaboration' in offices?
questions.explanation: Expert C calls the 'watercooler effect' a 'romanticized concept.' Expert D similarly dismisses the traditional focus on the office as an 'engine of productivity,' prioritizing leisure and culture instead.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the impact of remote work on small businesses in the city center?
questions.explanation: Expert A says the ecosystem of small businesses is 'collapsing.' Expert D disagrees, stating that 'those that adapt... will thrive' and the city will become a place for residents.
